title: Dativ (Dative case)
description: Dative case is often used for the indirect object (the person receiving something) or after specific prepositions like mit (with), bei (at/with), and zu (to).

sections.title: 1. The Core Transformation: How Articles Change
sections.description: In the Dative case, the articles (the/a) change their form based on the gender of the noun.

masculine_neutral.title: Masculine and Neutral (They share the same forms)
masculine_neutral.description: For masculine nouns (der) and neutral nouns (das), the definite article "the" becomes dem. The indefinite article "a" becomes einem, and "no/none" becomes keinem.

feminine.title: Feminine
feminine.description: For feminine nouns (die), the article "the" becomes der. The indefinite article "a" becomes einer, and "none" becomes keiner. This is often confusing for learners because der looks masculine, but in the Dative case, it is strictly feminine.

plural.title: Plural
plural.description: For plural nouns (die), the article "the" becomes den. There is no indefinite article for plural (you can't say "a books"), but the negative "none" becomes keinen.
plural.crucial_rule: In Dative Plural, you must also add an -n to the end of the noun itself (unless the noun already ends in -n or -s).

sections.title: 2. Using "Mit" (Transportation and People)
sections.description: The preposition mit always triggers the Dative case. This is why when you talk about how you travel or who you are with, the articles change.

content.logic: When you see mit, look at the gender of the vehicle or person following it.

sections.title: 3. Dative Verbs: Giving and Helping
sections.description: Some verbs automatically require the Dative case for the person involved because that person is the "receiver" of the action. Common verbs include helfen (to help), gratulieren (to congratulate), and schenken (to give a gift).

schenken_geben.description: When you give something to someone, the person is Dative and the object is Accusative.

examples.english: She gives the cat (die Katze) a fish.
examples.german: Sie gibt der Katze einen Fisch.

examples.english: She gives a baby (das Baby) milk.

sections.title: 4. Possessive Articles in Dative
sections.description: Possessive articles (my, your, her, etc.) follow the exact same ending patterns as the word kein or ein.

content.example_context: If you are at a party and someone arrives with their boyfriend, you say: Sie kommt mit ihrem Freund. (ihr + em because Freund is masculine Dative). If she arrives with her children, you say: Sie kommt mit ihren Kindern. (ihr + en + plural 'n').

sections.title: 5. Prepositions of Location (Bei, Zu, Von)
sections.description: Just like mit, the prepositions bei (at/with), zu (to), and von (from/of) always require the Dative case.

examples.translation: I am staying with the friends.
I am going to a lake - masculine

sections.title: 6. Summary of Article Endings
sections.description: To remember this easily without a chart, focus on the "Signal" letters at the end of the Dative articles:

signal_letters.meaning: M is for Masculine and Neutral

signal_letters.examples: deN / keineN) + add an N to the noun
